ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Prata Bomb; mapped to the nearest parent class 'flatbread food product' (FOODON_03315595). A more specific term for stuffed South Asian flatbread cooked with ghee is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Flat bread) is the closest defensible match. No specific code exists for Prata Bomb or stuffed roti prata variants in the FoodEx2 hierarchy as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Prata Bomb or roti prata as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Prata Bomb is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one whole stuffed prata piece as served at a Singapore hawker stall or Indian Muslim restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Prata Bomb — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Prata Bomb (cr_id G00010321). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken as reported. Saturated fat is estimated from the HPB entry for plain prata and standard ghee contribution, as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat for this variant; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Prata Bomb specifically. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the closest analogous food: white wheat flatbread / roti (GI ~70) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The high refined wheat flour content (75.23g carbohydrate/100g) and ghee-fried preparation support this estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (70 × 75.23 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 79.0 for a 150g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 632 mg per 100 g exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 948 mg sodium, representing 41% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Prata Bomb to occasional consumption, request minimal added salt and ghee during preparation, avoid high-sodium curry dipping sauces, and account for the sodium contribution within their daily 2300 mg ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load of 75.23 g/100 g with an estimated GI of 70 produces a glycemic load of approximately 79 per 150 g serving, causing a significant post-meal blood glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Prata Bomb due to its very high glycemic load; if consumed, pair with a high-fibre vegetable side and protein to partially blunt the glucose response, and monitor post-meal blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (estimated ~3.5 g/100 g) combined with high sodium (632 mg/100 g) and refined carbohydrate content elevates cardiovascular risk through multiple pathways including LDL cholesterol and blood pressure.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Prata Bomb to occasional small portions, request preparation with minimal ghee, avoid cheese-stuffed variants, and pair with low-sodium accompaniments to reduce combined saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 632 mg/100 g exceeds the RA threshold; ghee-derived saturated fat and refined wheat flour base promote pro-inflammatory mediators that may worsen joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of Prata Bomb; if eaten, request minimal ghee, avoid cheese fillings, and limit portion size to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake that exacerbates joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prata Bomb is a popular Singaporean Indian hawker dish consisting of a thick, stuffed roti prata — a layered wheat-flour flatbread of South Indian origin — cooked on a flat griddle with generous amounts of ghee (clarified butter). Unlike the standard plain prata, the 'bomb' variant is characterised by a thicker dough that is folded around a filling, most commonly egg, and sometimes cheese, creating a denser, softer, and chewier interior with a crisp, golden-brown exterior. The dough is made from refined wheat flour, water, salt, and ghee, which is also used liberally during cooking to achieve the characteristic flaky layers and rich flavour. Prata Bomb is typically served with fish or chicken curry dipping sauce and is a staple at Indian Muslim (mamak) stalls across Singapore. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 412 kcal per 100 g, with a high carbohydrate content of 75.23 g/100 g derived predominantly from refined wheat flour, making it a high-glycemic food (estimated GI ~70). Sodium is elevated at 632 mg/100 g, which is clinically significant for individuals managing hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis. Fat content is moderate at 8.25 g/100 g, with saturated fat contributed by ghee. Dietary fibre is relatively high at 10.2 g/100 g. The dish is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its very high glycemic load, nor for those with hypertension given its sodium content. It is generally considered halal and vegetarian when prepared without meat fillings.
common_user_queries: How many calories in prata bomb?
Is prata bomb high in sodium?
What is the difference between plain prata and prata bomb?
What is inside a prata bomb?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010321, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from standard ghee contribution to plain prata as a secondary approximation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white wheat flatbread as the closest match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) is estimated based on the known ghee content of standard roti prata preparation and USDA FoodData Central data for ghee; the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat for Prata Bomb. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for Prata Bomb are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific stuffed prata variant as of 2024. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source despite the egg filling, possibly reflecting the composite recipe average. 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate using white wheat flatbread from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Prata Bomb exists in the approved GI literature. 5. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'Malay'; this has been corrected to 'Singaporean Indian' as Prata Bomb is a dish of South Indian Muslim (mamak) origin widely consumed in Singapore. 6. Dietary fibre of 10.2 g/100 g as reported by HPB is notably high for a refined wheat flatbread and may reflect the composite recipe including filling ingredients; this value is preserved as reported by the approved source.
